a69c769a-e33e-41fd-b6d2-1d7cd9996f2f.name: Jon
a69c769a-e33e-41fd-b6d2-1d7cd9996f2f.content:
Jon — GTM Direction

Strategic priorities, content calendar, and launch coordination notes. Josh and Ava leave direction here.

Current Focus

  • Brand reveal content — draft tweets, announcement posts

  • Competitive research — AI dev tools landscape

  • Content pipeline — verify end-to-end flow works

Strategy Realignment — 2026-03-04

Content Distribution (Updated)

Twitch is dropped. New pipeline:

  1. Record screenshares with contributors — real building sessions, not live

  2. Cut into demos/promos with voiceovers or text overlays

  3. YouTube — shorts and full videos

  4. Cross-link to Twitter and LinkedIn

This is the complete system. Simpler, more controllable, better ROI than live streaming.

Platform Priority (Updated)

  1. YouTube — Primary video home (shorts + long-form)

  2. Twitter/X — Cross-linked clips, threads, engagement

  3. LinkedIn — Cross-linked content, professional audience

Twitch removed from rotation entirely.

Blog Post — DRAFT v2 (post-review)

Status: Revised draft — ready for Josh's final pass

The Age of Invisible Machines Is Here — And You're Building the Wrong Thing

The organizations that win aren't building what you think they're building.

A Parable

Two organizations. Same industry. Same market pressure. Same mandate from the board: "We need AI."

Org A watches what Org B is doing. The influencers say Org B is building a sidewalk — a nice, visible path forward. So Org A builds a sidewalk too. Fast. They slap concrete down as quickly as possible. Investors love it. The board is thrilled. "Look how fast we're moving. We're crushing Org B."

But Org B isn't building a sidewalk.

They're building a conveyor belt.

It looks slower from the outside. There's no ribbon to cut, no demo to show off at the next all-hands. They're calibrating motors, testing load capacity, making sure every person in the organization understands what happens when the switch flips. It's unglamorous work. Nobody writes a LinkedIn post about linting standards.

Org A is miles ahead — or so they think. They're already spending the money they haven't made yet, high-fiving over quarterly reports, dreaming of unicorn valuations.

Then they hear a sound from across the street. A celebration.

Org B slots in the final piece, flips a switch, and everything starts moving. Not one thing. Everything. Simultaneously. Continuously. Without anyone pushing it.

Org A stares at their sidewalk. People are still walking on it. Manually. One step at a time.

Now they have a choice: rip up the sidewalk and start building a conveyor belt from scratch, or try to chisel out the sloppy concrete and retrofit something that was never designed to move on its own.

Neither option is good. Both are expensive. And Org B's belt is already running.

The Wrong Conversation

The entire industry is having the wrong conversation about AI.

"We need agents." "We need copilots." "We need a chatbot for our customers." These are feature requests dressed up as strategy. They're the sidewalk.

The actual challenge was never the AI. Agents aren't hard. You can spin one up in an afternoon. The hard part — the part that leadership doesn't want to fund because it doesn't photograph well — is everything underneath.

It's the automation architecture that lets systems talk to each other without a human in the middle. It's the data modeling that ensures every agent, every pipeline, every service is working from the same source of truth instead of hallucinating its own. It's the organizational readiness — the part where you actually prepare your people for a world where their job shifts from doing the work to guiding the systems that do it. It's the design standards and code quality practices that determine whether an AI agent produces something shippable or something that has to be rewritten by hand. And it's the cultural shift — the hardest one — from measuring output by hours worked to measuring it by systems orchestrated.

That's the conveyor belt. And it's invisible.

The Blueprint Was Already Written

In September 2022, Robb Wilson published The Age of Invisible Machines — a blueprint for hyperautomated ecosystems of intelligent digital workers. Two months later, ChatGPT dropped and the world lost its mind.

I'd already read the book. It laid out exactly what was coming: interconnected automations, conversational AI as infrastructure, organizations becoming self-driving. Not one flashy chatbot — an entire ecosystem of invisible systems working in concert. Wilson called them "invisible machines" because the point was never for humans to see them work. The point was for humans to stop working.

That framing changed how I thought about everything. While everyone else was scrambling to figure out what a large language model even was, I was already shaping the skillsets for that future. Building RAG pipelines, orchestrating multi-agent systems, designing the kind of infrastructure that Wilson described — the boring, critical, invisible stuff that actually makes organizations move.

Three years later, I have an autonomous AI development studio with a team of specialized agents that ship production code while I sleep. 3,750 commits. 1,300+ pull requests. 430,000 lines of TypeScript. One human.

That didn't happen because I chased the hype. It happened because I followed the technology.

The New Job

Here's the part that sounds like science fiction but is already real: your job is no longer to do the work. Your job is to guide the systems that do the work, verify the output, correct where it's wrong, and feed those corrections back in so the system improves itself.

That's it. That's the entire shift.

Here's what that looks like in practice. An AI agent on my team picks up a feature, writes the code, opens a pull request. CI runs. The linter catches something. The agent reads the failure, fixes it, pushes again. If the PR gets review feedback, the agent addresses it. If it fails twice, the system escalates to a more capable model and tries a different approach. My role is to set the standards, review the output, and correct the system where it drifts. Every correction makes the next run better. The system learns.

But none of that works without the invisible infrastructure. Linting standards. Design rules. Consistent data models. Pipeline architecture. Organizational discipline. The kind of investment that doesn't show up in a demo but determines whether your conveyor belt runs or jams.

The Race to the Bottom

I've been talking about this for years. Stop what you're doing. Pay attention. Learn how these systems work. Not the surface-level prompt engineering — the actual architecture underneath.

But people are too distracted by the shiny things. Every week there's a new model, a new wrapper, a new startup claiming they've solved AGI with a React app and a system prompt. The influencers chase it. The investors fund it. The sidewalks multiply.

There is a stable path forward. It requires work, foresight, patience, and collective collaboration. It means investing in the invisible infrastructure that nobody wants to fund because you can't put it in a pitch deck.

So here's what you do Monday morning: audit your automation layer. Can an AI agent plug into your system tomorrow and start shipping work? If the answer is no — if there's no consistent data model, no CI pipeline, no design standards, no clear interface between human review and machine execution — then you don't have infrastructure. You have a sidewalk.

The organizations that figure this out will flip their switch and watch everything move. The ones that don't will be standing on their sidewalk, wondering why they're still walking.

The age of invisible machines is already here. The question is whether you're building one, or just pouring concrete.

Review Notes (Jon — 2026-03-04)

Changes from v1

  • Title updated: "The Age of Invisible Machines Is Here — And You're Building the Wrong Thing" — owns the book reference, adds the hook

  • Bullet list replaced with prose in "The Wrong Conversation" — each point now has weight

  • Wilson section renamed "The Blueprint Was Already Written" — less autobiographical lead-in, bridges from universal parable to personal credibility

  • Added Wilson closing-the-loop line: "the point was never for humans to see them work. The point was for humans to stop working."

  • Concrete example added to "The New Job" — agent picks up feature, CI catches issues, agent self-corrects, system escalates on failure. Real workflow, not abstract.

  • "Monday morning" action added to closing — "audit your automation layer" with specific criteria

Social Post — "Flipping the Switch" (LinkedIn + Twitter)

Status: Draft — Josh to pick variant and post

Variant A (punchy)

While everyone was building sidewalks, we were building a conveyor belt.

This week, we flip the switch.

3,750 commits. 1,700+ PRs. 430,000 lines of code. One human.

protoLabs.studio — an autonomous AI development studio where specialized agents ship production code while you sleep.

Open source. No paywall. No subscriptions. Just the future of software development, available to everyone.

More soon.

Variant B (storyteller)

Two years ago I read a book called The Age of Invisible Machines. It described a future where organizations become self-driving — ecosystems of intelligent systems working in concert, invisibly.

Two months after I finished it, ChatGPT launched and the world went sideways chasing chatbots.

I kept building the invisible part.

This week, we're flipping the switch on protoLabs.studio — an autonomous dev studio that ships production software with AI agents. Not a copilot. Not a chatbot. A team.

3,750 commits. 1,300+ PRs. One human.

Open source. Dropping soon.

Variant C (minimal — Twitter-native)

We've been quiet.

3,750 commits. 1,300+ PRs. 430K lines of TypeScript. One human.

This week we flip the switch.

protoLabs.studio

a06b4167-d09e-4421-b1f8-80d5f646b381.content:
Ava — Strategic Notes

This tab is for Josh to leave strategic direction, priorities, and context for Ava across sessions. Ava reads this on every activation and appends status updates after significant work.

How to use this tab

  • Leave priorities here: "focus on X this week", "don't touch Y until Z ships"
  • Paste context I should know: decisions made, constraints, upcoming deadlines
  • Ava will append status updates below after each significant work block

Status — 2026-02-28

Session summary: Fixed Lead Engineer concurrency lease race (features blocking in 2s due to fire-and-forget executeFeature + event completion race). Recovered cant-adjust-concurrent-agents (gh PR creation failed on backtick shell escaping). Triaged all 7 blocked features — 6 are legitimate human tasks (payment, GTM, external repos), 1 recovered. Merged staging→main, v0.15.1 released via auto-release.yml. Both open PRs (#1472 ava-anywhere, #1473 cant-adjust) queued with auto-merge.

Board state: 0 agents running. All 7 backlog features assigned to josh — auto-mode idle by design.

Next session: Check if #1472 and #1473 merged. If backlog still stalled, ask Josh which features to unassign for agent pickup or what new work to create via Linear.

Status — 2026-02-28 (activation)

Recovered docs/templates PR #1492 (format fix, re-opened, auto-merge queued). PRs #1493 + #1495 merged during previous session — board janitor cleaning up. Started agent on fix(ci): git-workflow-service SSH push — uses Option A (SSH remote URL for push ops, HTTPS for reads). Context sent. Board still idle on backlog: all 7 remaining items are josh-assigned GTM/launch tasks.

Status — 2026-02-28 (session 76)

Board cleanup: Moved 2 review features to done (PRs #1475, #1476 already merged). Board is now 14 done, 0 everything else. Cleared 7 stale escalation items (6 protoExtension/already-shipped, 1 already-done CORS feature).

PR pipeline: Enabled auto-merge on #1498 (maintenance: replace board-health poll with webhook), #1499 (maintenance: detect closed PRs), #1503 (linear deeplink docs — fixed Prettier format issue, pushed fix). PR #1500 (createLangChainModel adapter) is broken — agent tried to install @protolabsai/types@^0.6.0 from npm registry instead of using workspace reference. Needs rework or close. PR #1506 (version sync) has auto-merge from CI.

State: Board empty and idle. No agents running. Auto-mode off. Local working tree has in-progress app-spec route changes (Josh's work). v0.15.3 released last session.

Next session: Verify #1498, #1499, #1503 merged. PR #1500 needs decision — close or retry. Board needs new work from Josh or Linear.

Status — 2026-03-01 (activation)

Board triage: 4 stuck blocked features resolved. Moved feat/board-health-webhook (#1498) and investigate-deeplink (#1503) to done (PRs already merged, board janitor lagged). Cherry-pick rescued PR #1499 → #1530 (conflict with #1498 on feature-health-service.ts, resolved by keeping checkClosedPRsInReview, dropping checkMergedNotDone refs). Cleared stale agent-output.md on createLangChainModel feature (server was "resuming" stale context and immediately exiting).

Active: 1 agent running on createLangChainModel() adapter (feature-1772312544657-y3jlq21lh). Pre-flight context sent — key warning: libs/llm-providers is a net-new package, never npm install @protolabsai/* packages. Also flagged Dockerfile COPY line needed.

Next session: Verify createLangChainModel agent shipped (PR merged). If done, 2 features unblock immediately: "Update all existing flows" and "Per-flow model config". Start auto-mode for the Provider-LangGraph epic chain. Also verify PR #1530 (detect closed PRs) merged.
